sentences.text: Yes, we cleaned the streets in the-- this was-- this was just, you know, to make-- to make us low, you know, to-- to make you feel that you are, you know, not the same as you used to be before.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] later, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for this--
This happened before they took us in the ghetto, before they took us in the ghetto.
And later-- we-- we heard-- we heard already that in Stepan is already they-- they made a ghetto for the Stepan Jews.
And they start to take from all around, from the villages, the rest of the Jewish people.
I mean from Krychylsk.
It was one-- village was Krychylsk.
The other village was Verbche.
You see, around us, they took already from Krychylsk.
They took from Verbche.
So we knew that any day they're going to come, and they're going to take us.
One day they came-- came two Germans.
And they told us, you have one-- one hour.
You are going to the ghetto.
Take whatever you can for that hour.
And it's no more than one hour.
So must take is the bedding, you know?
But what can you take for an hour, and how much it was-- it was a-- a horse and wagon.
It was so many people asked, what can you take there?
I mean, and the bedding is already.
So and we were afraid.
So we took what we can.
We took the bedding.
And people, the children who couldn't walk, they went to the wagon.
And they told us that you walk after the wagon.
If somebody tried to make a move, you're going to be shoot-- shot on the spot.
They brought us to the ghetto, and no-- they show us where to-- and the ghetto was very crowded.
They took from the whole city one street, one-- the smallest street.
And they gathered the street with the wooden-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wooden fence.
And from the whole city, the people have to be in that one-- one street.
It was very crowded.
It was maybe 15, 20 families in-- in one house, maybe more.
People used to sleep on the-- on the floor, and wherever-- it's like-- like herring.
Can you imagine what-- what happened that time, and so many people.
But people, you know, people were together.
You know, it was a time that people didn't argue.
They-- it was-- I mean, there's still the situation.
We have to do the best we can.
So the ghetto was divided in two.
It was-- the ghetto, they divide it in half.
One half was for the children, the mothers and the children.
The other half for the-- for the men.
The men-- are not supposed to come.
God forbid they caught, they can shoot you for coming to the-- that side.
In the beginning, when I came, my brother-in-law, he used to be-- he was in the men side.
So he took me there.
My mother and the children, took on the other side.
So but I was-- my age they couldn't tell.
You know, I belong here or I belong here.
So sometimes I was with the mother.
And sometimes, later I was-- in the beginning I was there.
There was like-- you know, wooden beds, make like that, like barracks.
You know, because it was there to sleep, with a little bit of straw.
It was like barracks.
So I was there for a while.
And it was-- [NON-ENGLISH] how it was.
But anyway, I was-- most of the time I was with mother later.
I was about 16, 15 or 16.
So later, the food was very bad.
They used to give us soup like water, and a piece of bread.
But everybody in that-- there was a Oberjuden, you know, it was-- it was the head of the-- of the ghetto.
It was the Jewish-- it was the Judenrat, you know.
And the Jewish police, they used to-- the didn't have a-- a weapon, but they give them sticks.
So they have to see, you know, that the men are not going to the-- and if-- if somebody wants to-- wants a Jew to work, they have to go to the-- to the Judenrat, and pay 15 mark, Deutsche Mark.
And then he can take you, the Christians from the outside.
If somebody from the Christians wants somebody to work, he went, he bought a Jew.
A lot-- a lot of Christians that were-- they were our friends, you know?
We used to-- used to be a time that we were friends.
They used to buy just to do you a favor.
Who was on the Judenrat?
Did you know the people who belonged-- who made up the Judenrat?
Yossel Wax was the Oberjuden, Yossel Wax.
And there, I don't know the rest.
And I remember the Oberjuden.
They used to take people to clean the streets, you know.
It was already a brigade.
You know, they have every day they have to-- this is from the Judenrat.
They decided who to take.
And so I-- I was in that.
I used to go-- the ladies or everybody used to go and sweep the streets.
And I used to go with a-- a-- barrel wagon-- how you call?
A wheelbarrow, I used a wheelbarrow, and collect the trash.
So this was my steady job.
I used to go every morning.
Sometime it was good for, because I could do, you know, a favor to people.
Some people-- it was no food there, the food what they used to give.
But some people, you know, hide something, you know, by the Christians.
But they couldn't bring in, in the ghetto.
So when I used to go with that wagon-- with that wheelbarrow, I had the opportunity.
They told me where.
And I took some food.
I put in that, and cover with that, you know, trash or whatever it was there.
And so it was very danger.
I used to go near the ghetto.
I knew already where people are waiting, and throw.
This was-- this was danger if they would catch me.
But I do it for people, you know.
I do it for our family too, you know.
It was very danger.
But everything was danger at that time, whatever you do.
How did you get the food?
Did you have ration cards, or did-- what was the system for giving-- for giving food to a family.
No, it was no cards.
It was-- I don't remember already.
Twice a day, they used to give you a little soup.
The soup-- what kind of soup can-- can they give you?
It's whatever they couldn't use already, they're going to give to the Jews.
And that was a little bit imitation broth, a piece of bread.
But some people, you didn't have a choice.
You have to live.
But who-- some people, you know, before they went to the ghetto, they had friends, Christian friends.
They had-- you know, they left something there.
They would, you know.
We were ourselves, before they took us in the ghetto, we hide some things, you know, in the barn.
We just hide them.
You know, we make a hole, and we-- we covered this in the dirt.
Some things they find.
And some things they didn't find.
So one time, my brother-in-law, he has Christians that he used to deal with them.
He was very, very good terms.
So he took one, and we went back to Korost, nighttime, you know, nobody sees.
It was very dangerous things.
We came to-- and we find things, what-- what we had there.
At that time was soap.
Soap was something that you couldn't buy for money.
We had soap.
So we-- and that Christian who hauled us in his-- and we used to-- used to sell to somebody, and for some food, you know.
In this way, we used to-- matter things.
But it was for a while later, my brother-in-law came.
He said that the Christian said that somebody steal that.
So that was that.
How did you get out at night?
You see, we used to-- it was very-- the fence were very high.
But you have to leave, you have to-- so we-- we put a piece of wood here, you know, you can step with the-- with a foot to jump over.
So this way, we had our places.
And from the other side was a garden, you know, a garden.
So you can hide there.
We try everything just to-- just to survive.
What was some-- clothes-- you know, clothes.
We had the-- we have-- my sister was ready for marriage.
So they prepared for her, you know, you know, for the-- for a girl, so we had good things.
We had-- we have good things there, so we hide in this everything, whatever we had we used to-- we used to sell just for-- you know, to survive.
No, not a lot of times.
It was very danger.
But we went only one time for-- you know, to Korost.
We just-- this was a miracle that we came back.
But we-- we saw things what we-- we have a lot of things they find, you know, the-- the neighbors, you know.
It was not-- it was empty, you know, They could do everything they wanted.
But something they didn't find, because it was very good cover, so-- this was the story.
It's a lot to talk about it.
But people used to-- a lot of times people used to jump over the fence.
They got killed, you know?
It was-- it happened very often.
What did some of the other members of your family work-- do in the ghetto?
What did they do in the ghetto?
I-- I don't think they did-- they didn't do anything, you know.
It was not-- for the women, there was nothing to do there.
I mean, they had plenty to do in the house like that where you have 15 families there, you know.
But, you know, no-- there was no special work for them.
The best, if somebody-- if they find somebody outside the ghetto, the best thing if you get-- they used to take in the [NON-ENGLISH].
They used to beat him up, you know, give him 25.
It was 25 sticks, you know.
He used to come.
He used-- it was already a certain way.

But one-- one time [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my-- my brother-in-law, he used to-- people used to buy him out of-- just to do him a favor, you know, to-- to feed him, to-- to be out there, outside the ghetto.
He came over.
He was like black, like, you know, it was-- I saw his face, something going on.
He came over.
He said that a Christian, a Ukrainian told him that they ordered 500 wagons and horses for tonight to the ghetto.
And they-- they think that this is for the Jews.
This was already-- it's already 1942. 1942, this was the-- it is a lot more to the-- probably a lot I didn't-- you know, you cannot.
But I came to that story.
So he came.
He told everybody.
It's no good.
Somebody told him that they ordered 500 wagons and horses.
And people start to gather, and you know, what's going to be?
I remember I was still a child, and I remember.
I used to go and listen what people are talking about.
I heard some people say, ah, yes.
Here's trouble.
The Russians, they pushing the Germans.
The Germans are in trouble.
The Russians are pushing them.
So probably they want to take out some goods, you know, with them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So he said, no, it doesn't look to me like that.
But some people-- you know, people don't want-- they want to be optimistic.
They don't want to think that it's-- that's the last day.
But maybe-- maybe.
And then the people start too.
But everybody was already sure that something is coming.
So everybody, what we're going to do, how to do what.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So we were running-- I miss something, what I want to tell you.
I miss something.
I-- I came back to that.
Before-- before they decided to-- to kill everybody, it was an order.
All the men from 16 and older has to-- has to come out on the middle of the ghetto, and they're going to be-- they're going to take them to-- they take them to work.
But we knew already what can work, and what-- of course, we heard what happened.
We-- we knew that in Rovno they used to make acts-- they used to go, whoever they catch on the street, 10,000 at one time, you know, whatever-thousand people.
[INAUDIBLE], they used to disappear.
The people used to kill them.
So but, no other choice.
Everybody get out.
Everybody was ready to go.
They took them away.
They took away two-- my two brothers.
It was three brothers, Josef, Aaron, and Monia.
They took them away.
So we thought this is-- this is the end.
But later we find out, you know, people have connections, that they are in Gestapo.
They are working for the [NON-ENGLISH].
They are building their swimming pool.
They do other things.
This was-- this was to-- to divide the families, to take away the-- the men from the women.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So that was a short time.
It was not-- some way, I don't remember, my brother Josef came back to the ghetto.
He escaped from there.
How he managed to do it, I don't know.
And he was hiding in the ghetto.
Even-- you know, I told that I'll come back to the other story.
So in that-- in that night, when they ordered the wagons, you know, Josef was with us.
And the two other brothers were in gestapo.
So-- so, we were what to do.
You know, people didn't know what to do.
People was looking-- were looking for hiding places, for where can you hide, you know, where can you hide, how to escape, how to.
And my mother said-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] children, you have to run.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe somebody will be alive.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was very hard [SOBBING] to leave the family.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Josef told me, let's run.
Let's run.
[PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
So we tried to run to the fence.
But wherever you go already, they feel you are close, you know.
They started-- they already-- we are surrounded.
They start to shoot, and they threw a grenade over.
You don't know where to go.
So you run back.
So all night we try here.
We try here.
We couldn't.
All is like-- like a front, you know, shooting wherever people start to do something.
Until it was midnight, or maybe 2 o'clock.
We had-- and we heard all night long with the wagons.
It was stones, you know, the rattles from stones make.
And the wagons were coming, coming, all night long, coming.
But all of a sudden we heard, it was the-- when we used to be for-- I don't know-- for dinner or for lunch, there used to be a bell they used to-- so a signal.
So where to-- we heard already the wagons start to come in-- in the ghetto.
And the Germans start to-- and that bell, alarm, alarm.
You know, such an alarm, alarm!
So this is-- we saw already.
Before we're thinking, maybe, maybe, maybe that, maybe that.
So, it it might have said [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] One sister has a small child.
The other sister has two children.
But, Josef said, let's run.
He came over.
We start to run.
We heard already an alarm that they're coming in.
We come to the-- the fence.
Josef jumped over.
I after him.
There was-- there was Germans, Ukrainians, and who knows what they were.
We jump straight on them.
They were-- and they, halt, halt, halt.
And they start to shoot after us, shoot after us.
But it was still dark, and it was, you know, a guard.
And you know, we run away.
We run away towards Korost.
Because we knew there the people.
We knew where can we run.
If isn't there, we know maybe somebody will.
So we run away.
There was Horyn.
It was like a river, a very deep river, a Horyn.
And there was bushes around the Horyn.
The Horyn goes from Stepan to Korost, you know, over-- so we-- until we came to that-- to that Horyn, we saw somebody-- one Christian.
He feed his horses.
He say, hey.
If you had already there, it's like a front, you know, shooting like a front.
Where are you going, Jews?
Where you go?
Back, back.
He want us back.
So my brother said, here is my shoes.
Let us leave.
No.
Back.
But I saw [INAUDIBLE].
So I-- I was ready to kill him.
And I thought I'm going to-- So he saw my reaction.
He-- he took his horses.
He say, I'm going to catch you, anyway.
I'll come with the Germans after you.
We run away.
We run away.
There were already-- it was already morning, you know.
You know, all the Christians, they feed the horses and the cows.
And they-- so we met one-- what we used to deal with him, you know, my father was-- they were friends, you know.
We used to make business, some kind of business.
He said, what happened?
They kill you.
They kill already?
They're going to kill already?
We said, yeah.
He said, maybe you can do some-- go, just run.
They were afraid to look at you.
Just run.
So-- but he-- we knew one, what he was in the-- in-- his house was in the woods, you know.
He was in the woods.
And it was not around, near houses.
We came to him.
He was-- he's not very reliable.
But we have no other choice, you know.
So somebody like that that can risk.
He knew that we were-- so we came to him.
And we--
